title: STANDARD FOR DRIED OR DEHYDRATED ROOTS, RHIZOMES AND BULBS TURMERIC CXS 359-2024

sections.section: STANDARD FOR DRIED OR DEHYDRATED ROOTS, RHIZOMES AND BULBS TURMERIC CXS 359-2024
sections.summary: Document title: Codex Standard for Dried or Dehydrated Roots, Rhizomes and Bulbs — Turmeric (CXS 359-2024); this is the standard's title rather than a substantive regulatory provision.
Administrative note stating the standard was adopted in 2024.

Scope: standard applies to dried or dehydrated plant products classified as spices (including turmeric) offered for direct consumption, as food ingredients, or for repackaging, and excludes product intended for industrial processing.

Defines dried/dehydrated turmeric as the product from drying primary (bulbs) or secondary rhizomes (fingers) of Curcuma longa L. and specifies permitted styles (whole/fingers, cut/pieces, or powdered), with other distinct styles allowed if labelled accordingly.
Definition: dried or dehydrated turmeric is obtained from drying primary or secondary rhizomes of Curcuma longa L. (Curcuma domestica Valeton) as identified in the accompanying name table.

Permitted styles for dried or dehydrated turmeric are whole/fingers, cut/pieces (various diced or sliced forms), and powdered/ground, with other markedly different styles permitted provided they are labelled accordingly.
Composition requirement: dried or dehydrated turmeric must conform to the compositional requirements specified in Annex I.

Quality factors: the product must have characteristic sensory properties (odour, flavour, colour) free from foreign off‑notes, comply with Annex I chemical and physical requirements, and, if traded as classified, Annex I serves as the minimum requirement.

Sensory requirement: the product shall have characteristic odour, flavour and colour (which may vary with geoclimatic conditions) and be free from foreign odours/flavours or colours, especially rancidity and mustiness.

Chemical and physical requirement: dried or dehydrated turmeric must comply with the chemical and physical specifications in Annex I (Tables A1 and A2), and permitted defects must not impair appearance, keeping quality or package presentation.
Optional classification: when turmeric is traded as classified, the chemical and physical characteristics in Annex I apply as minimum requirements.
Food additives: anticaking agents listed in Table 3 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) are permitted for use in ground/powdered turmeric conforming to this standard.

Contaminants: products must comply with relevant Codex limits and guidance, including the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995), the Code of Practice for prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices (CXC 78-2017), and Codex pesticide maximum residue limits.

sections.section: 5.1 The products covered by this standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the General standard for contaminants  and  toxins  in  food  and  feed (CXS 193-1995), 2  the Code  of  practice  for  the  prevention  and reduction of mycotoxins in spices (CXC 78-2017), 3  and other relevant Codex texts.
sections.summary: Obligation to comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995), the Code of Practice for mycotoxin prevention/reduction in spices (CXC 78-2017), and other relevant Codex texts.

sections.section: 5.2 The products covered by this standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Products must comply with Codex‑established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that the products covered by this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with relevant Codex hygiene texts (e.g., CXC 1-1969; CXC 75-2015; Annex III on spices and aromatic herbs) and that products comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
Containers shall be filled as fully as practicable without impairment of quality and must be consistent with a proper declaration of contents.

Requires labelling in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and adds specific provisions on product name, origin/harvest information, commercial identification and non‑retail container labelling.
The product common name must follow Section 2.1, may include an indication of style per Section 2.2, and may list trade name, variety or cultivar on the label.

sections.section: 8.3 Country of origin and country of harvest
sections.summary: Country of origin must be declared; country of harvest is optional; region of harvest and year of harvest are optional.
Label may indicate class/grade if applicable, or may optionally indicate size.
Labelling of non-retail containers should follow the Codex General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

For compliance testing, the methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to this standard shall be used, while a sampling plan is noted as to be developed.
Specifies that methods of analysis and sampling contained in CXS 234-1999 relevant to the standard’s provisions shall be used for checking compliance.
Non-substantive: the sampling plan is not yet developed (stated as 'To be developed').
Non-substantive: bibliographic notes listing FAO/WHO and Codex references cited in the standard (e.g., CXS 192-1995, CXS 193-1995, CXC 78-2017, CXC 1-1969, etc.).
